title: Ms. Knope Goes To Washington

jokes.text: That building looks like a boob.
jokes.setup: Leslie is waxing poetic about D.C.'s grandeur and government
jokes.punchline: Andy reduces the Capitol building to a crude visual comparison
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy moment - his childish perspective cutting through pretentious moments. Good economy and character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The Capitol building is a symbol of everything that's great about this country. The dome, the marble columns, the sense of history and importance...

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It looks like a really big penis.

jokes.text: Yeah, well, it's not. Oh, yeah, I know. It's the White House. No. It's the Capitol.
jokes.setup: Andy thinks the Capitol looks like a boob
jokes.punchline: His geography confusion escalates - he can't even identify basic D.C. landmarks
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Andy's geographic ignorance, well-integrated with his character's consistent stupidity.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, the Capitol building looks like a boob.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It doesn't look like a boob, Andy.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: The dome is totally a boob. You're telling me the founding fathers built a giant boob in the middle of our nation's capital and nobody noticed?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not... that's not what it looks like.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: What about the Washington Monument? That's obviously a... you know.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: A what?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A giant... it's phallic, Leslie. Our capital is basically a dirty joke.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, those are architectural landmarks that represent—

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: And the Lincoln Memorial? Two giant stone legs. Very subtle, guys.

jokes.text: Now, throw it away, because Leslie Knope is your guidebook. I didn't... I didn't mean literally. I had some notes in it.
jokes.setup: Leslie makes a metaphorical statement about being Andy's guidebook
jokes.punchline: Andy takes it literally and throws away the actual guidebook with her notes
jokes.explanation: Well-constructed misdirection that perfectly captures both characters - Leslie's over-enthusiasm and Andy's literal-mindedness.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm like your guidebook.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh, okay.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Then I don't need this anymore.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: *throws away actual guidebook*

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, no, no, Andy!

jokes.text: There's a mall? That's awesome. I need to get some flip-flops.
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions the National Mall (a historic park)
jokes.punchline: Andy thinks it's a shopping mall and wants to buy flip-flops
jokes.explanation: Classic Andy misunderstanding, economical and perfectly in character, though predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: The National Mall is one of the most historic parks in the country.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, there's a mall? I need to go get some flip-flops.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: No, Andy, it's not a shopping mall. It's a national park.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh. Well, that's disappointing.

jokes.text: You're going to throw the Leslie Knope Employment Enjoyment SummerSlam Grill Jam Fun-splosion?
jokes.setup: Ron says he's throwing the annual barbecue
jokes.punchline: The ridiculously over-the-top name Leslie gave to a simple barbecue
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Leslie's over-enthusiastic naming conventions. The absurd length and alliteration make it memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm throwing the annual barbecue.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: You mean the Pawnee Harvest Festival Celebration Barbecue Extravaganza?

jokes.text: Parks and Dolls! I got your park right here / Its name is Ramsett Park / And its gates are open from dawn till dark
jokes.setup: Reference to Leslie's one-woman show about parks rules
jokes.punchline: A musical theater parody with bureaucratic lyrics
jokes.explanation: Clever 'Guys and Dolls' parody that turns mundane park information into musical theater. Original and well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: ♪ Parks and Rec, it's the best in the laaand ♪

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: ♪ You gotta fill out Form 27-B before you use the sand ♪

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: ♪ Submit it in triplicate, notarized and signed ♪

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: ♪ Leave it at the office, we'll review it in time ♪

jokes.text: Barbecues should be about one thing. Good shared meat.
jokes.setup: Ron explaining why he won't do Leslie's elaborate barbecue activities
jokes.punchline: His reductive, meat-focused philosophy delivered deadpan
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - his libertarian minimalism applied to barbecues. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I don't understand why we need all these activities. We have meat. We have fire. We cook the meat on the fire. That's a barbecue. Everything else is just... noise.

jokes.text: Well, Ron, can we at least have corn on the cob? No.
jokes.setup: Jerry asks for a seemingly reasonable barbecue food
jokes.punchline: Ron's immediate, flat rejection because corn is a vegetable
jokes.explanation: Ron's anti-vegetable stance taken to its logical extreme. Great economy - the joke is in the immediate 'No.'

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Can I bring corn to the barbecue?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No. Corn is a vegetable.

jokes.text: How do I know this isn't a treasure map just waiting to be unfurled? Because it's gum. That's gum.
jokes.setup: Andy finds something stuck to the Lincoln Memorial
jokes.punchline: He insists ordinary gum could be a treasure map despite obvious reality
jokes.explanation: Andy's National Treasure obsession leading him to see conspiracies in mundane objects. Character-consistent but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Whoa, whoa, whoa! This could be a treasure map!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Andy, that's gum.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Gum that's been here for like a hundred years. That makes it old gum. Old things are valuable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: It's literally just gum stuck to a monument.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A monument to treasure hunting!

jokes.text: Leslie, this is a really cool penis but Ben and April are meeting us at the Smithsonian in 10 minutes.
jokes.setup: Leslie is giving educational tour of Washington Monument
jokes.punchline: Andy reduces the phallic monument to crude terminology while she's being serious
jokes.explanation: Juvenile humor that fits Andy's character but relies on obvious visual comparison. Cheap but character-appropriate.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: And this is the Washington Monument, built to honor our first president, George Washington. It's one of the most iconic structures in our nation's capital.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's a big... you know.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes, Andy, it's a monument.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, I mean it's like a giant... it's a big ol' dong.

jokes.text: Way to be, Ron! You're really getting it done, man! He can't hear me.
jokes.setup: Chris enthusiastically cheering on Ron at the barbecue
jokes.punchline: Revelation that he's talking to himself because Ron is too far away
jokes.explanation: Classic Chris moment - his relentless positivity even when it's pointless. Good character integration and structure.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You're doing great, Ron! That's perfect form! The way you're holding that spatula is really inspiring! Keep it up!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Ron? Ron, did you hear me? I said your form is inspiring!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: He can't hear me, can he? He's like fifty yards away.

jokes.text: His name is Tom. Burn. Seriously? No, I understand that it's hilarious. But that is his given Christian name.
jokes.setup: Ron introduces the pig they're going to eat, named Tom
jokes.punchline: Tom's reaction and Ron's deadpan explanation that it's coincidentally also Tom's name
jokes.explanation: Excellent layered joke - the coincidence, Tom's reaction, and Ron's matter-of-fact response create multiple laugh moments.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, the pig's name is Tom?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Yes. It's a coincidence.

jokes.text: Which one of you youngsters wants to help me drain the blood from this animal? If you do a good job, I'll give you the bladder. You can blow it up for a fun play ball.
jokes.setup: Ron trying to involve children in the barbecue
jokes.punchline: Graphic description of pig slaughter presented as a fun children's activity
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly dark humor - Ron's survivalist worldview horrifying modern parents. Original and perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Now children, gather around. I'm going to show you how we turn this pig into delicious meat. First, we need a sharp knife and a steady hand. You see, when you slit the throat, you want to cut deep and quick so the pig doesn't suffer. Then we'll hang it up by the hind legs and let all the blood drain out into this bucket. Next comes the fun part—we scald it in boiling water to remove the hair and skin. Then we use this cleaver to split open the belly and remove all the organs. Each one has a specific purpose. The liver, the kidneys, the heart—all delicious. And don't worry about the squealing sound it makes, that's just air escaping from the lungs. Who wants to help me gut it?

jokes.text: This just says, 'I can do what I want.' I am the director of the Parks Department and this is a park.
jokes.setup: Ron shows his permit to slaughter the pig
jokes.punchline: It's not a real permit, just his libertarian philosophy written down
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson joke - his anti-government stance taken to absurd logical conclusion. Highly quotable and original.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's not a real permit, just my libertarian philosophy written down.

jokes.text: Sir, you protect our nation's history and you're a hero. Yeah. A hero with something to hide. Like probably a treasure map.
jokes.setup: Andy compliments a museum security guard
jokes.punchline: Immediately turns suspicious and accuses him of hiding treasure maps
jokes.explanation: Good callback to Andy's conspiracy theories, showing his inability to maintain normal social interactions.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what? You're doing a great job. Really impressive work here.

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Oh, uh... thanks?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, seriously. The way you stand there, the alertness, the uniform. You're like a human sentinel.

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: Well, I appreciate that.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Which is why I have to ask — where are you hiding the treasure maps?

dialogue.speaker: Security Guard
dialogue.line: What? I'm not hiding anything.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: A guy as thorough as you? A museum this old? There's definitely treasure maps. And you know where they are.

jokes.text: We were going to have sex. Okay.
jokes.setup: Leslie wants to sightsee with Ben and April
jokes.punchline: April's blunt announcement of their plans cuts through Leslie's enthusiasm
jokes.explanation: Classic April deadpan delivery creating awkwardness. Great economy and perfect character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh my gosh, we should go to the Ritz-Carlton and get spa treatments, and then we can hit up the Natural History Museum, and then—

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: We're going to sit in the hotel room and watch TV.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? No, April, we have so much to see and do!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm aware of what exists, Leslie. I choose not to experience it.

jokes.text: Were you talking to my butt? Yes.
jokes.setup: Ben says goodbye to April
jokes.punchline: April calls out his inappropriate gaze with typical directness
jokes.explanation: April's blunt honesty creating an uncomfortable moment. Economical but relies on crude humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Goodbye, April.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Hey, Ben? Your eyes are on my butt.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: What? No, they're not.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: They are. It's okay. But they are.

jokes.text: It feels like I just exercised. Just sit on the ground. No, Jerry. It's dirty and I'm wearing my summer linens.
jokes.setup: Jerry complaining about being tired from minimal walking
jokes.punchline: His prissy refusal to sit on the ground reveals his vanity
jokes.explanation: Shows Jerry's contradictory nature - physically weak but vain about appearance. Character-consistent but not particularly funny.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I'm exhausted. I walked all the way from the parking lot.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Jerry, that's like fifty feet.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I know. My dogs are barking. I need to sit down.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Just sit on the ground.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: On the ground? With all this dirt and... groundness?

jokes.text: That's not a picnic blanket. That's a merino wool throw for my Eames chair.
jokes.setup: Ann brings out what she calls a picnic blanket
jokes.punchline: Tom's pretentious correction about his expensive home furnishing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom materialism - his obsession with expensive designer items even in casual situations.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: That's not a picnic blanket, that's a Hermès throw.

jokes.text: Ann and Tom, A.K.A. Haverkins, is stronger than ever.
jokes.setup: Donna questioning their relationship status
jokes.punchline: Tom's desperate creation of a celebrity-couple portmanteau name
jokes.explanation: Tom's pop culture obsession creating fake relationship branding. The name 'Haverkins' is memorably awkward.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: So what are we?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We're Tomna.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Donnom?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: No.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Ton?

jokes.text: Let's move in together. Totally. No take-backs when we sober up, though, partner. No take-backs, partner.
jokes.setup: Flashback to their drunk decision to move in together
jokes.punchline: Their overly serious commitment to a terrible drunken idea
jokes.explanation: Standard setup for the relationship deception storyline. Functional but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: We're doing this. We're moving in together. Right now.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Tom, we've known each other for like three hours.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Exactly. So we better lock this down before you come to your senses.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm going to come to my senses in like five minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Then we have to sign the lease before that happens.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: We can't sign a lease right now. We're drunk.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Even better. We won't remember how stupid this is.

jokes.text: More importantly, I bet Donna $1,000 that we'd be together for another month. I can't stress this enough. If she finds out that we broke up, I'll go bankrupt.
jokes.setup: Tom explaining why they're pretending to still be together
jokes.punchline: His financial desperation raising the stakes of the deception
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Tom's financial irresponsibility creating real consequences for his lies.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Look, I need this. My credit is shot, my car payment is three months behind, and I'm pretty sure my landlord is going to evict me. So yes, we're staying together.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: At least until I can figure out how to tell my mom that I'm not actually engaged anymore. She already posted about it on Facebook.

jokes.text: Tommy's got the tum-rums.
jokes.setup: Tom complaining about being hungry
jokes.punchline: His childish made-up word for stomach rumbling
jokes.explanation: Tom's tendency to create cutesy phrases for everything. Character-appropriate but simple wordplay.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: My stomach's doing that thing again. That... growly-wowly noise.

jokes.text: You can get water from that water fountain and use it to water down the beer. Why not just give the kids water? I suppose you could do that.
jokes.setup: Chris asks what children should drink at the barbecue
jokes.punchline: Ron's suggestion to water down beer rather than just give them water
jokes.explanation: Perfectly captures Ron's libertarian parenting philosophy and complete disconnect from modern child safety.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: What should we give the kids to drink at the barbecue?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Water it down beer. Why waste good beer on kids when you can just give them water?

jokes.text: But by the time this day is over, they will have been taken by the grill and delicately and tenderly shown the ways of flavorful meat-love.
jokes.setup: Ron talking about teaching people to appreciate grilled meat
jokes.punchline: His romantic, sexual language applied to barbecuing
jokes.explanation: Ron's passionate relationship with meat described in romantic terms. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Ron Swanson
dialogue.line: When you grill a steak, you're not just cooking meat. You're engaging in an ancient ritual that connects you to your ancestors, to the land, to the very essence of what it means to be alive. The sizzle of fat hitting hot iron, the smoke rising like incense to the gods of carnivore heaven. You caress the meat with your tongs like you're touching the skin of a lover. You whisper to it. You tell it that everything is going to be okay.

jokes.text: Ben and I are a power couple, like the Roosevelts or the Clintons... Oh, wait. We do. Three, four...
jokes.setup: Leslie comparing herself and Ben to famous political couples
jokes.punchline: They have a signature dance move that she demonstrates
jokes.explanation: Leslie's political ambition mixed with dorky relationship enthusiasm. Visual gag we can infer from the count-in.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben and I are like the Obamas, or the Clintons, or any other power couple in history. We even have a signature dance move.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: *demonstrates an awkward dance move*

jokes.text: Oh. Most people just mail their applications in. I'm not most people.
jokes.setup: Receptionist explains the normal application process
jokes.punchline: Leslie's confidence that her personal approach makes her special
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's naive belief that her enthusiasm will overcome bureaucracy. Character-appropriate but predictable.

dialogue.speaker: Receptionist
dialogue.line: You fill out the form, wait six to eight weeks, and we'll review your application.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yeah, but I'm going to get it done in like two weeks because I'm going to call you every single day and bring you waffles and find out what your favorite song is, and then I'm going to write it on the form so when you see my application you'll be like, 'Oh, this girl gets me.'

jokes.text: You know, there's a CD inside that plays the sound of a babbling river... I was also going to show a DVD that had some images of frolicking river otters.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her special application materials
jokes.punchline: Her overly elaborate multimedia presentation for a simple grant application
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie over-preparation, turning bureaucratic paperwork into a multimedia experience.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've prepared a multimedia presentation for this grant application. It includes a 47-minute interpretive dance sequence, a holographic recreation of our town's founding, and a original song cycle performed by the Pawnee Community Theater.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, it's a $500 grant application.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know. That's why I kept it simple.

jokes.text: Is that Pawnee, Illinois, Indiana, Iowa, Kansas, Missouri, Nebraska, Oklahoma, or Texas?
jokes.setup: Leslie mentions her town is called Pawnee
jokes.punchline: There are apparently eight different Pawnees, showing how generic/unimportant her town is
jokes.explanation: Good reality check on Leslie's assumption that her town is important or memorable to outsiders.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: There are apparently eight different Pawnees.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Yeah, there's Pawnee, Indiana, Pawnee, Oklahoma, Pawnee, Illinois...

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Okay, so my entire town's identity is completely generic.

jokes.text: Ben, you're fired. It's my office now. Boom!
jokes.setup: April sees Ben's impressive office space
jokes.punchline: Her immediate decision to fire him and take over
jokes.explanation: Classic April power move - her ambition expressed through typical blunt directness.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: You're fired. I'm taking your office.

jokes.text: Could we just real quick stop at the nearest place that has free pants?
jokes.setup: Learning the party requires pants instead of shorts
jokes.punchline: Andy's assumption that free pants are available somewhere
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - his financial situation and naive worldview creating an absurd question.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, so you're telling me I have to wear pants? Like, real pants?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But where am I supposed to get pants? Is there like a pants station or something? Do they just hand out free pants?

jokes.text: I smuggled in some candy. Oh, thank God. I'm starving. Raisins? It's nature's candy.
jokes.setup: Jerry offers 'smuggled candy' to hungry Tom
jokes.punchline: It's just raisins, which he calls 'nature's candy'
jokes.explanation: Classic Jerry moment - his pathetic version of rebellion and healthy eating presented as exciting.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: I've got some smuggled candy if you're hungry.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Smuggled candy? Yes, please.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: It's raisins. Nature's candy.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Jerry, raisins are just dried grapes. That's not candy, that's... that's just sad.

jokes.text: Did you put glitter in the laundry detergent? Oh, Yeah. I'm experimenting with some new entrepreneurial ideas. That one is called 'SparkleSuds. Dress Loud.'
jokes.setup: Ann discovers glitter in her laundry
jokes.punchline: Tom's ridiculous business idea with equally ridiculous slogan
jokes.explanation: Tom's terrible business ideas with over-the-top marketing slogans. 'Dress Loud' is memorably bad.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Tom, there's glitter all over my clothes. What did you do?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Oh, that's my new business venture: Glitter Gal. We bedazzle your entire wardrobe while you sleep.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's insane. What's your slogan?

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Glitter Gal: Because you didn't ask for it, but you're getting it anyway.

jokes.text: This morning, you put glitter in the butter. 'Disco Dairy. Spread the Party.' No, that's not a good idea. That's terrible.
jokes.setup: Ann listing Tom's other glitter products
jokes.punchline: Even more ridiculous product concept with punny slogan
jokes.explanation: Excellent escalation with 'Disco Dairy. Spread the Party.' - the pun and concept are hilariously bad but memorable.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You've got glitter condoms, glitter lube, glitter body spray...

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Glitter toilet paper. The slogan is 'Leave a Sparkly Surprise.'

jokes.text: I love politics. Look. I made a shrimp claw. I'm really proud of you, babe. Now, put your used plate in my purse. I almost have a complete set.
jokes.setup: Andy's innocent enjoyment of the fancy party food
jokes.punchline: April's proud response followed by revealing they're stealing plates
jokes.explanation: Great contrast between Andy's childlike joy and April's criminal enterprise, both perfectly in character.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: These little pigs in a blanket are amazing! And the crab cakes? Forget about it!

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I know. I've been taking them all night.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Wait, what? You're stealing them?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Not just the food. The plates too.

jokes.text: My grandma is on the city council in her town. It gives her a reason to leave the house.
jokes.setup: Guest saying local government is important
jokes.punchline: Reveals she sees it as charity work for elderly people
jokes.explanation: Shows the dismissive attitude toward local government that Leslie constantly faces. Well-structured putdown.

dialogue.speaker: Party Guest
dialogue.line: Local government is really important.

dialogue.speaker: Party Guest
dialogue.line: I see it as charity work for elderly people.

jokes.text: And I was mistaken for Beverly D'Angelo by a Japanese tourist.
jokes.setup: Leslie comparing her day's accomplishments to Ben's
jokes.punchline: Her 'achievement' is being mistaken for a random actress
jokes.explanation: Leslie's tendency to inflate minor incidents into major accomplishments. The specificity of Beverly D'Angelo makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Today I got mistaken for Jennifer Aniston at the grocery store.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's nice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I know! I've accomplished so much.

jokes.text: I gave them to the kids. They love them. They're skipping them across the pond.
jokes.setup: Chris asks about his vegan soy patties
jokes.punchline: Ron gave them to children to use as skipping stones
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron anti-vegetarian sentiment - finding the most disrespectful use for vegan food. Excellent character integration.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: What happened to those vegan soy patties I brought?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I gave them to some children to use as skipping stones.

jokes.text: Ron, if no one takes me to the bathroom, I'm just going to have to go in the trees.
jokes.setup: Jerry has been asking for bathroom help
jokes.punchline: His threat to urinate in the trees as final resort
jokes.explanation: Jerry's pathetic desperation creating uncomfortable humor. Character-appropriate but crude.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: If you don't help me to the bathroom right now, I'm going to urinate in the trees.

jokes.text: Does anybody feel like they can't breathe? I think I need some fresh air. We're outside.
jokes.setup: Leslie having an anxiety attack at the party
jokes.punchline: She needs fresh air despite already being outside
jokes.explanation: Shows Leslie's anxiety through her illogical thinking. Good economy in the contradiction.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I need some fresh air.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, we're outside.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I need fresher air!

jokes.text: It's like C-SPAN and Neiman Marcus had kids or something.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing the intimidating D.C. women
jokes.punchline: Combining politics and high fashion into one metaphor
jokes.explanation: Clever metaphor combining Leslie's political obsession with her intimidation by fashionable women. Highly quotable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: These D.C. women are like Machiavelli in a pencil skirt. They smile while they're cutting you down, they network at charity galas, and they somehow make political betrayal look like it's from a Vogue photoshoot.

jokes.text: We're overrun with raccoons and obese toddlers.
jokes.setup: Leslie describing Pawnee's problems to the Senators
jokes.punchline: Combining mundane and absurd problems in one statement
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie self-deprecation - her tendency to over-share Pawnee's embarrassing problems when nervous.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: We have a pothole the size of a small car, our parks are underfunded, and there's a duck that won't leave the community center pool. It's been there for three weeks and I think it might be planning something.

jokes.text: I thought you'd enjoy meeting numbers 4 and 26 on Leslie's List of Amazing Women.
jokes.setup: Ben trying to cheer up Leslie after she meets the Senators
jokes.punchline: Reveals Leslie has a numbered list of amazing women
jokes.explanation: Perfect insight into Leslie's organizational mind - of course she has a numbered list of role models.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Hey, you know what? You're an amazing woman. You're smart, you're passionate, you care about people.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thanks, Ben.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: And you know what else? You're not alone. There are tons of amazing women out there doing incredible things.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Really?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Yeah. I mean, look at—

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have a list. Women I admire. It's numbered. I've been working on it since high school. I'm up to 247.

jokes.text: I put glitter in all your moisturizers and lotions. I'm calling it 'SparkleSkin by Annie. Twinkle, Twinkle, Big Star.'
jokes.setup: Tom's glitter business ideas continue
jokes.punchline: Another terrible beauty product with worse slogan
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of Tom's terrible business ideas. 'Twinkle, Twinkle, Big Star' is memorably bad marketing.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay, okay, hear me out. 'Glitter Cheeks' — it's a blush that's 60% actual glitter.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: That sounds like a skin condition.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: The slogan is 'Sparkle Like You've Made Better Life Choices.'

jokes.text: As someone who has lied a lot about various aspects of a myriad of relationships, I respect the effort you've gone to.
jokes.setup: Donna revealing she knows Tom and Ann broke up
jokes.punchline: Her casual admission to being a chronic relationship liar
jokes.explanation: Perfect Donna moment - her mysterious personal life revealed through casual confession of chronic dishonesty.

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: Oh, I know. Tom told me you two broke up. He was very upset.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Wait, when did he tell you that?

dialogue.speaker: Donna
dialogue.line: I don't know, a while ago. I lie about stuff all the time, so I can't really keep track of when people tell me things.

jokes.text: I combined all of the giant, dark-haired smartphone power Goddesses into one woman called Hot Rebecca.
jokes.setup: Leslie explaining her jealousy about Ben's D.C. life
jokes.punchline: Her creation of a composite rival woman with specific physical attributes
jokes.explanation: Brilliant insight into Leslie's neurotic mind - creating an imaginary perfect rival. 'Hot Rebecca' is a perfect name choice.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I created a composite woman in my head. She's got Natalie Portman's face, Scarlett Johansson's body, and Michelle Obama's arms.

jokes.text: Everything is going to be fine with you and Ben because if I know Ben, he, too, is an amalgam. No. Yeah. Point is you're better than Hot Rebecca. You're Kickass Leslie. Nope. ...an amalgam. Nailed it.
jokes.setup: Andy trying to comfort Leslie with her own logic
jokes.punchline: He completely misunderstands the word 'amalgam' but is proud of using it
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - learning a new word but using it completely wrong while being proud of himself. Great callback structure.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey, look, I know you're upset about the merger, but think about it — it's like an amalgam of two great things coming together.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's... actually not a terrible way to look at it.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Right? And you know what an amalgam is? It's like a llama, but with more letters. A-M-A-llama. I'm basically a genius.

jokes.text: My nomination was 'Jerry accidentally forwards his bank statement to everyone.' Classic. And depressing.
jokes.setup: Chris mentioning funny email chains for the puppet show
jokes.punchline: Jerry's financial information becoming office entertainment
jokes.explanation: Typical Jerry humiliation becoming office entertainment. Chris's 'and depressing' shows awareness of the cruelty.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know what? I've been forwarding around some really funny email chains about the puppet show.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: Oh, that's great!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Yeah, but then I realized I accidentally forwarded your entire financial portfolio to the whole office.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: What?!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: And now everyone's been forwarding it around. It's become more popular than the puppet show emails.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry
dialogue.line: No, no, no...

jokes.text: Oh, my God. The smell is so much worse when you get up close. Yeah. Oh! Look. A handgun! I call it!
jokes.setup: Leslie and Andy starting to clean the polluted river
jokes.punchline: Finding a gun in the water and Andy claiming it like treasure
jokes.explanation: Good escalation of how polluted the river is, with Andy's childish 'I call it' reaction to finding a weapon.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Oh my god, is that a gun?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Don't touch it, that's evidence.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Finders keepers!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's not how the law works.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm calling it. This gun is mine now. I'm gonna name it Mr. Whiskers and we're gonna be best friends.

jokes.text: The White House, America's most whitest house. Most notably is where Sinbad lived in the film, First Kid.
jokes.setup: Andy giving a tour of D.C. landmarks
jokes.punchline: His terrible grammar and focus on a bad '90s movie instead of history
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy tour guide - bad grammar and pop culture references instead of actual history. 'Most whitest' is memorably wrong.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Welcome to the Washington Monument, folks. This thing was built in 1884, and it's the tallest structure in D.C. Fun fact: it's also where they filmed that awesome scene in Independence Day where the alien ship blows it up. Will Smith is so cool.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: Actually, the monument was completed in 1884, and Independence Day came out in 1996.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, the point is it's really tall and stuff, and that movie had way more history in it than this building, to be honest. Next stop: the Lincoln Memorial, where Lincoln give his famous speech about, uh, doing stuff.

jokes.text: Do you know why it's called the Oval Office? No. Because of a man. Oval Redenbacher. What? The popcorn inventor. He used to hang out with John F. Kennedy.
jokes.setup: Andy explaining the Oval Office name
jokes.punchline: Completely made-up story connecting Orville Redenbacher to JFK
jokes.explanation: Brilliantly absurd Andy explanation mixing real names with completely fabricated history. 'Oval Redenbacher' is a perfect malapropism.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So the Oval Office is called that because it's shaped like an oval, right? But here's the thing - most people don't know the real origin story. It actually comes from Orville Redenbacher.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: See, JFK loved popcorn. Like, loved it. And Orville Redenbacher was his favorite brand. So one day, JFK is eating popcorn in the Oval Office, and a kernel pops and flies into his ear. And he's like, 'This office is shaped like a popcorn kernel!' So they renamed it the Oval Office. Oval like a popcorn kernel.

dialogue.speaker: Tourist
dialogue.line: That doesn't sound right.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's 100% true. I learned it at the visitor center.

jokes.text: This tour guide is the most amazing tour guide there ever was. Please. Step up. $200 cash up front.
jokes.setup: Leslie praising Andy's terrible tour
jokes.punchline: Andy immediately trying to capitalize with an outrageous fee
jokes.explanation: Good escalation showing Andy's opportunistic nature and Leslie's supportive blindness to his incompetence.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That was an amazing tour, Andy. Really incredible.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Thank you! You know what, I'm going to start charging for these tours.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Five thousand dollars per person.

tmdb_overview: Leslie and Andy visit Ben and April at their new jobs in Washington D.C., and Leslie feels overwhelmed by the city -- especially after meeting two of her idols. Meanwhile, Ron throws a "thank you" barbeque for the entire Parks and Recreation staff.
insight: Leslie's Washington move triggers 52 character-driven jokes but lands softer than peak Pawnee seasons.
